text: The surgeon retracts the left lobe of the liver and begins the procedure by grasping and separating the gastroesophageal fat pad from the stomach.

The surgeon uses a white-tipped ligature device to cauterize and cut through blood vessels attaching the gastroesophageal fat pad to the stomach, with the assistant approaching from the patient's right side.

The surgeon continues to remove the gastroesophageal fat pad to expose the apex of the stomach, ensuring a clear view of the fundus. The hiatal hernia is visible, with the left and right crura of the diaphragm and a space between them, indicating the need for future repair.
The surgeon completely removes the gastroesophageal fat pad to expose the angle of His, the apex of the stomach where it angles into the esophagus.

The surgeon, having gained access to the left crux of the diaphragm and the angle between the stomach and esophagus, begins dissecting along the greater curvature of the stomach using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon palpates the pylorus to identify its location, then identifies an area between four and six centimeters proximal to the pylorus along the greater curve of the stomach.
The surgeon begins cauterizing through the blood vessels that attach the stomach to the colon and spleen, using a ligature device to separate these structures.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device, known as the ligature device, to cauterize tissue and enter the lesser sac, located behind the stomach.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealing device to cauterize large blood vessels. The assistant helps by splaying the vessels, allowing the surgeon to cauterize them individually for effective bleeding control.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the short gastric vessels near the spleen, slowing down to individually cauterize each vessel to prevent retraction and control potential bleeding.

The surgeon carefully navigates through the blood vessels located at the apex of the stomach, mindful of potential complications such as bleeding and splenic injury during the sleeve gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon exposes the backside of the stomach and the left crus of the diaphragm, having already removed the anterior fat pad for better visualization. The surgical field shows the diaphragm clearly visible in front of the stomach.
The surgeon achieves adequate exposure of the diaphragm, fully revealing the stomach fundus to prevent leaving a large posterior fundus in the surgical area.
The surgeon identifies a significant hiatal hernia in the patient and begins the dissection to open the space both anteriorly and posteriorly to the stomach.

The surgeon enters the space between the diaphragm and esophagus, separating these structures while encountering a small hernia sac. The assistant gently exposes the tissue to facilitate the opening of the hiatal hernia space.

The surgeon exposes the left cruciate diaphragm and begins to expose the right cruciate diaphragm, revealing the distal esophagus. The greater curve of the stomach is mobilized in preparation for sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon passes a 34 French bougie sizing tube down the esophagus into the antrum of the stomach. Using a tri-stapler, the surgeon begins creating the sleeve gastrectomy by placing the first staple line, which consists of three rows of staples on each side, cutting down the center to ensure an adequate seal and control of bleeding.

The surgeon begins the staple line for the vertical sleeve gastrectomy, with the assistant holding the stomach in a natural position. The stapling device is used to place staples vertically alongside the bougie, completing the sleeve formation.

The surgeon staples across the apex of the stomach, leaving a small portion of stomach tissue, known as a 'dog ear,' to avoid stapling across the esophagus. The staple line is carefully placed on the gastric wall.
The surgeon begins to over-sew the staple line using a simple Vicryl suture, applying a running, inverting technique from the top to the bottom of the staple line.
The surgeon completes the suture line by over-sewing the vertical sleeve gastrectomy in a running, inverting fashion, ensuring the staple line is securely reinforced.

The surgeon addresses a hiatal hernia by exposing the esophagus and the left and right crura of the diaphragm. The apex of the gastric sleeve is visible. A suturing device called an endostitch is used to repair the hernia, allowing precise suture placement without tissue injury.

The surgeon uses a suturing device to pass a permanent suture through both crura of the diaphragm, closing the posterior part of the diaphragm. The closure of the posterior diaphragmatic repair for the hiatal hernia is assessed for any remaining space anteriorly.

The surgeon identifies a small anterior space after the posterior diaphragmatic repair and places a figure-of-eight permanent suture to close the anterior diaphragmatic defect. The suturing device facilitates rapid and accurate placement of the suture, completing the repair both anteriorly and posteriorly.

The surgeon removes the esophageal bougie, which was used to create a narrow sleeve. Following its removal, the apex of the stomach, referred to as the 'dog ear,' is tacked to the diaphragm to prevent sleeve herniation into the mediastinum or chest.

The surgeon observes the narrow sleeve gastrectomy and the repaired hiatal hernia, with the apex tacked to the left crux of the diaphragm. An upper endoscopy is performed to test the integrity of the repair.

The assistant covers the surgical area with saline while the surgeon inserts a flexible Olympus endoscope down the esophagus into the lower stomach. The endoscope reveals a distended sleeve with no air leakage or internal bleeding. The scope is visible from the outside, and after confirming the lumen is adequate, the surgeon removes the air from the stomach and withdraws the endoscope.
The surgeon places one or two placating sutures between the omental fat and the lateral aspect of the sleeve to prevent twisting and potential obstructions.
The surgeon removes the suture material, revealing a straight gastric sleeve without twisting. A fibrin sealant is applied along the suture line from the apex downwards to prevent leaks.
The surgeon removes the liver retractor and locates the stomach specimen. The apex of the narrow stomach is grasped and extracted through a laparoscopic incision, concluding the procedure.
